videoTitle: They Promise Jesus, But Give You Paul (Why Christianity Is a 'Bait & Switch' Trick)
thesis: Protestant Christianity is a 'bait and switch' that draws people in with Jesus but centers Paul's teachings instead.
steelman: Abke's strongest argument is that many Protestant churches functionally center Pauline soteriology — justification by faith, substitutionary atonement, imputed righteousness — while underemphasizing the synoptic Jesus's direct teachings on ethics, forgiveness, and the kingdom of God. This tension is real and has been noted by scholars from Schweitzer to Sanders. The observation that a new convert might hear more sermons on Romans than on the Sermon on the Mount in many evangelical churches is an empirically defensible claim, even if Abke's framing as a deliberate 'bait and switch' and his specific textual arguments are frequently inaccurate.

falseClaims.claim: Jesus never taught that his death was a sacrifice for sins or that believing in his death and resurrection is the path to salvation.
falseClaims.evidence: Mark 10:45 — 'The Son of Man came not to be served but to serve, and to give his life a ransom for many.' Mark 14:24 — 'This is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many.' Matthew 26:28 adds 'for the forgiveness of sins.' These are Jesus's own words in the synoptic Gospels, not Pauline additions.
falseClaims.settle: The synoptic texts themselves, which directly attribute sacrificial language to Jesus.

sources.label: Mark 10:45 — ransom for many
Mark 14:24 — blood of the covenant poured out for many
Matthew 26:28 — for the forgiveness of sins

falseClaims.claim: Paul invented the concept of atonement through blood sacrifice — this idea is absent from Jesus's teaching.
falseClaims.evidence: The Last Supper tradition (Mark 14:22-25, parallels in Matthew and Luke) explicitly frames Jesus's death in sacrificial covenant language. This tradition is independently attested in multiple sources and predates Paul's letters chronologically in terms of the events described.
falseClaims.settle: A historical argument showing the Last Supper institution narrative was a Pauline invention inserted into the Gospels — no mainstream scholar argues this for Mark's version.

sources.label: Last Supper — sacrificial covenant language in the synoptics
Mark 14:22-25 — institution narrative

falseClaims.claim: Paul never quotes or references Jesus's ethical teachings — he only cares about the death and resurrection.
falseClaims.evidence: Romans 12:14 echoes Jesus's command to bless those who persecute you (Matt 5:44). Romans 12:17 parallels 'do not repay evil for evil' (Matt 5:39). Romans 13:8-10 echoes the love commandment. Romans 12:20-21 quotes Proverbs but applies Jesus's enemy-love teaching. 1 Corinthians 7:10-11 explicitly cites 'the Lord' on divorce. 1 Corinthians 9:14 cites 'the Lord' on worker's wages.
falseClaims.settle: The Pauline texts themselves, which demonstrably echo or directly cite Jesus's teachings.

sources.label: Romans 12:14-21 — Paul echoing Jesus's ethical teachings
Romans 13:8-10 — love fulfills the law
1 Corinthians 7:10-11 — Paul citing Jesus on divorce

falseClaims.claim: Jesus taught that salvation comes through works — doing good deeds, loving your neighbor — not through faith or belief.
falseClaims.evidence: While Jesus emphasizes ethical behavior (Matt 25:31-46, the Sheep and Goats), he also speaks of faith repeatedly: 'Your faith has saved you' (Luke 7:50), 'Your faith has made you well' (Mark 5:34, Mark 10:52). The synoptic Jesus integrates faith and ethics rather than opposing them. Framing Jesus as works-only requires suppressing his faith statements.
falseClaims.settle: The synoptic passages where Jesus attributes salvific power to faith.

sources.label: Matthew 25:31-46 — Sheep and Goats, ethical judgment
Luke 7:50 — your faith has saved you
Mark 5:34 — your faith has made you well

falseClaims.claim: The early church before Paul had no concept of Jesus as a sacrifice — this was Paul's theological invention.
falseClaims.evidence: 1 Corinthians 15:3-5, which Paul explicitly identifies as a pre-Pauline creed he 'received' from others, states 'Christ died for our sins in accordance with the scriptures.' This creedal formula is widely dated to within 3-5 years of the crucifixion by scholars across the theological spectrum (Hengel, Dunn, Fee), predating Paul's own theological development.
falseClaims.settle: Scholarly dating of the 1 Corinthians 15 creed — consensus places it in the earliest stratum of Christian tradition.

sources.label: 1 Corinthians 15:3-5 — pre-Pauline creed
1 Corinthians 15 creed — scholarly dating discussion

falseClaims.claim: Paul contradicts Jesus on the law — Jesus upheld the law while Paul abolished it.
falseClaims.evidence: Both positions are more nuanced. Jesus upholds the law (Matt 5:17-20) but also radicalizes and reinterprets it (Matt 5:21-48 antitheses), declares all foods clean (Mark 7:19), and heals on the Sabbath. Paul says the law is 'holy and righteous and good' (Rom 7:12) but argues believers are not under its condemnation. The relationship between Jesus and Paul on the law is a genuine scholarly debate, not a simple contradiction.
falseClaims.settle: A scholarly consensus on whether Jesus and Paul are contradictory or continuous on the law — no consensus exists, which itself disproves the claim of simple opposition.

sources.label: Matthew 5:17-20 — Jesus on fulfilling the law
Romans 7:12 — Paul affirms the law is holy
Mark 7:19 — Jesus declares all foods clean

falseClaims.claim: Paul's letters predate the Gospels, which proves the Gospels were written to conform to Paul's theology.
falseClaims.evidence: Paul's letters (c. 50-60 CE) predate the written Gospels (c. 65-95 CE) — this dating is correct. However, the conclusion does not follow. The Gospels draw on oral traditions, Q source material, and earlier written sources that are independent of Paul. Mark's Gospel in particular shows minimal Pauline influence according to most scholars. Chronological priority of writing does not establish theological dependency.
falseClaims.settle: A scholarly argument demonstrating that Mark's Gospel was theologically shaped by Paul — this is a minority position (Goulder) and not the consensus.

sources.label: Synoptic Gospels — dating and sources
Q source — pre-Pauline Jesus tradition

falseClaims.claim: The book of James directly contradicts Paul and was written as a polemic against Pauline theology — 'faith without works is dead.'
falseClaims.evidence: James 2:14-26 does use language that appears to oppose Paul's 'justified by faith' formulation. However, many scholars argue they are using 'faith' and 'works' with different definitions and addressing different problems. Paul opposes works of the law as a basis for justification; James opposes empty intellectual assent without behavioral change. Whether this constitutes a direct contradiction or a complementary emphasis is debated.
falseClaims.settle: Scholarly consensus on the James-Paul relationship — genuinely divided, which itself makes the claim of 'direct contradiction' an overstatement.

sources.label: James 2:14-26 — faith and works
Epistle of James — relationship to Pauline theology

entries.quote: Jesus never taught that his death was a sacrifice for sins.
entries.explanation: Presents the claim as absolute while concealing Mark 10:45, Mark 14:24, and Matthew 26:28 where Jesus uses explicit sacrificial language.

entries.quote: Paul never quotes or references Jesus's ethical teachings.
entries.explanation: Conceals Romans 12:14-21, Romans 13:8-10, 1 Corinthians 7:10-11, and 1 Corinthians 9:14 where Paul explicitly cites or echoes Jesus.

entries.quote: Jesus taught salvation through works, not faith.
entries.explanation: Conceals multiple synoptic passages where Jesus attributes salvific power to faith: Luke 7:50, Mark 5:34, Mark 10:52.

entries.quote: Christianity is a bait and switch trick — they promise you Jesus but give you Paul.
entries.explanation: The 'trick' framing is designed to provoke emotional reactions from Christian viewers, making them defensive rather than engaging analytically with the theological argument.

entries.quote: Paul was a self-appointed apostle who never met Jesus and contradicted everything Jesus taught.
entries.explanation: Attacks Paul's credentials and character rather than engaging with the substance of his theological arguments. Omits that Paul's apostleship was recognized by the Jerusalem leaders (Gal 2:9).

entries.quote: If you believe Paul's theology, you're essentially saying Jesus's own teachings weren't sufficient for salvation.
entries.explanation: Extends the Pauline position into an extreme it does not hold. Paul never claims Jesus's teachings are insufficient — he builds on them within a post-resurrection theological framework.

entries.quote: This is a bait and switch — the oldest con in the book.
entries.explanation: Classifies the theological relationship between Jesus and Paul under a pejorative label ('con') to dispose of the argument without engaging its defenders (Augustine, Luther, Calvin, Wright).

scholarlySplits.claim: Paul's theology represents a departure from Jesus's original message
scholarlySplits.nature: A genuine scholarly debate. Schweitzer, Wrede, and the Religionsgeschichtliche Schule argued for significant discontinuity. Dunn, Wright, and Hengel argue for fundamental continuity within a post-resurrection framework. The 'Paul vs. Jesus' debate has been active since the 19th century with no resolution.

scholarlySplits.claim: The 1 Corinthians 15 creed is pre-Pauline and dates to within years of the crucifixion
scholarlySplits.nature: Broad scholarly consensus (Hengel, Dunn, Fee, Fitzmyer) dates the creed to 3-5 years after the crucifixion. A minority (Price, Carrier) questions its pre-Pauline origin. This is one of the stronger scholarly consensuses in New Testament studies.

scholarlySplits.claim: James 2 directly contradicts Paul on faith and works
scholarlySplits.nature: Luther famously called James an 'epistle of straw.' Catholic and many Protestant scholars argue James and Paul are complementary, using terms differently. Some critical scholars (Dibelius) see genuine tension. The relationship remains actively debated.

scholarlySplits.claim: The Gospel of Mark shows Pauline theological influence
scholarlySplits.nature: Goulder and a minority argue Mark reflects Pauline theology. The majority view (Hengel, Marcus, Collins) sees Mark drawing on independent pre-Markan tradition with minimal Pauline influence. The debate continues but the majority position opposes Pauline dependency.

trueClaims: Paul's letters (c. 50-60 CE) are the earliest Christian documents, predating the written Gospels
Many evangelical churches functionally center Pauline soteriology in their preaching
The Sermon on the Mount is often underemphasized relative to Romans in Protestant teaching
Paul identifies himself as a Pharisee and former persecutor of the church
The book of James emphasizes works alongside faith — 'faith without works is dead'
Jesus taught extensively about ethics, forgiveness, and the kingdom of God in the synoptic Gospels
The rich young ruler passage (Mark 10, Matt 19, Luke 18) shows Jesus pointing to commandment-keeping
Jesus's summary of the law is love of God and love of neighbor (Matt 22:37-40)
Paul's Damascus road experience is described differently in the three Acts accounts
The Protestant Reformation was heavily influenced by Paul's letter to the Romans
Martin Luther's 'justification by faith alone' derives primarily from Pauline texts
There is genuine scholarly debate about continuity vs. discontinuity between Jesus and Paul
The 'already but not yet' eschatological tension exists in both Jesus's and Paul's teaching
Jesus told parables about judgment based on behavior — sheep and goats, talents, wise and foolish virgins

youtubeFlags.text: The title 'They Promise Jesus, But Give You Paul' frames an ongoing scholarly debate as a settled deception. The 'bait and switch trick' language implies deliberate fraud rather than theological development.

The video implies that anyone who attends a Protestant church is being deceived. This framing bypasses the scholarly complexity of the Paul-Jesus relationship and presents one interpretation as the hidden truth.

Systematically omits synoptic passages where Jesus uses sacrificial language (Mark 10:45, Mark 14:24, Matt 26:28) and passages where Jesus emphasizes faith (Luke 7:50, Mark 5:34). The omissions are not acknowledged.
References to courses, membership programs, and educational offerings are present throughout the content.

weightedAnalysis.interpretation: At 46%, this session is significantly misleading. The false claims are concentrated in the core thesis — specifically the denials that Jesus used sacrificial language and that Paul engaged with Jesus's ethics. These are not peripheral errors but foundational claims that the entire video argument depends upon. Background facts about dating and church history are largely accurate.

Jesus never taught that his death was a sacrifice for sins
Paul invented the concept of atonement through blood sacrifice
Paul never quotes or references Jesus's ethical teachings

Paul contradicts Jesus on the law — Jesus upheld it while Paul abolished it
Paul's letters predate the Gospels which proves the Gospels conform to Pauline theology
The book of James directly contradicts Paul and was written as a polemic against him
The Sermon on the Mount is underemphasized relative to Romans in Protestant preaching
Paul identifies himself as a former Pharisee and persecutor of the church

Jesus's summary of the law is love of God and love of neighbor

The Protestant Reformation was heavily influenced by Paul's letter to the Romans
Luther's justification by faith alone derives primarily from Pauline texts
There is genuine scholarly debate about continuity between Jesus and Paul
Jesus told parables about judgment based on behavior, not belief
Paul was recognized by the Jerusalem church leaders as an apostle
The 1 Corinthians 15 creed predates Paul and reflects the earliest Christian belief
Jesus's teaching on forgiveness — if you do not forgive, you will not be forgiven — is radical and ethical
Paul's theology replaced Jesus's simple ethical message with complex doctrinal systems
The already-but-not-yet eschatological tension exists in both Jesus and Paul

Your faith has saved you — Jesus said this to the sinful woman in Luke 7
Mark 10:45 has Jesus saying he came to give his life as a ransom for many
Romans 12:14-21 shows Paul echoing Jesus's ethical commands about enemy love
The Q source contains Jesus traditions independent of Paul
Paul's Damascus experience transformed a persecutor into the greatest missionary in history
If you believe Paul you are saying Jesus's teachings were not sufficient for salvation
Christianity is the oldest con — promising one thing and delivering another
The institutional church has always prioritized Paul over Jesus for control purposes
James the brother of Jesus led the Jerusalem church and represented Jesus's original teachings
The Didache — an early Christian document — reflects a Jesus-centered ethical Christianity without Pauline soteriology
